one_line_takeaway: CacheProbe empirically demonstrates that OpenRouter's default shared-organizational-credential routing breaks per-account prompt-cache isolation on OpenAI, Groq, and Fireworks alike -- via timing side channels and cached_tokens metadata disclosure -- while BYOK mode restores it, pinpointing shared upstream credentials (not the gateway concept itself) as the root cause.
key_finding: Direct-API baseline: all three providers show correct cross-account isolation (Groq cross p=8.3e-03, Fireworks cross p=6.1e-01, OpenAI cross p=6.9e-01, no cross-account cached-token reports), while same-account caching is confirmed functional (Groq same p=3.8e-11, Fireworks same p=3.8e-11, OpenAI same via metadata disclosure). OpenRouter default mode: cross-account cache sharing detected for all three providers -- Groq shows 100% metadata cache-hit-rate disclosure in both same- and cross-account OpenRouter scenarios despite a non-significant KS p=1.7e-03 for cross; Fireworks shows the study's strongest timing signal, cross-account p=4.08e-15 (KS stat 0.364, miss median TTFT 0.395s vs hit median 0.363s); OpenAI shows minor leakage via metadata, 4.8% of cross-account requests reporting cached tokens above the prefix threshold. OpenRouter BYOK mode: isolation is restored for all providers/scenarios, cross-account p-values all > 0.16 and no cross-account metadata cache-token reports -- confirming the leak is caused specifically by OpenRouter's shared organizational provider-side credentials rather than by the routing/proxy layer per se.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is CacheProbe, an empirical audit showing that OpenRouter's API gateway architecture breaks provider-level prompt-cache isolation. Building on the timing-side-channel technique of Gu et al. (ICML 2025, cited as [1], which the paper explicitly attributes as prior work), the authors design and run a 6-scenario x 3-provider experimental matrix (Direct Same/Cross account, OpenRouter-default Same/Cross account, OpenRouter-BYOK Same/Cross account) against OpenAI (gpt-4o-mini), Groq (openai/gpt-oss-20b), and Fireworks (qwen/qwen3-8b), collecting 250 interleaved miss/hit sample pairs per scenario (~9,000 total API requests, Nov 12-Dec 8, 2025, from an Oracle Cloud VM in US-East). They combine a timing-based Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (threshold p < 1e-8, following [1]) with a metadata-disclosure check on the API's cached_tokens field (positive if >50% of cache-primed requests report >90% of the expected prefix length as cached). The core empirical finding is that all three providers correctly isolate cross-account caches when accessed directly, but all three leak cache state across accounts when routed through OpenRouter's default (shared-organizational-credential) mode, and isolation is restored when using OpenRouter's BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) mode. The paper attributes the vulnerability specifically to OpenRouter pooling user traffic under shared provider-side credentials, not to the routing layer itself, and documents a responsible-disclosure attempt to OpenRouter (contacted Nov 14, 2025 and Dec 12, 2025; OpenRouter acknowledged but did not follow through on requested repo access).
threat_model: Adversary is an authenticated OpenRouter user (valid API key with credits) attempting to detect or reconstruct prompt prefixes submitted by other OpenRouter users. The adversary can force routing to a specific upstream provider via OpenRouter's provider.order parameter and measure Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) or read cached_tokens metadata on its own requests. The adversary needs no access to OpenRouter internals, the victim's credentials, or the victim's account -- only a standard OpenRouter account. Detecting a cache hit indicates another user recently submitted a prompt sharing that prefix, and per the cited prior work [1], such detection can be escalated into token-by-token prompt reconstruction via iterative prefix probing.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: only three providers and one model per provider were tested (OpenAI gpt-4o-mini, Groq openai/gpt-oss-20b, Fireworks qwen/qwen3-8b), so isolation behavior may differ across other providers/models on OpenRouter; prompts were randomly generated ASCII-letter sequences rather than realistic application prompts, which may not reflect real-world cache hit patterns; cache eviction policy and timing may vary with provider load or time of day, uncontrolled for; and the conservative statistical thresholds may miss partial or intermittent cache sharing. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the text): model selection was constrained by OpenRouter compatibility (e.g., gpt-4.1-nano was excluded because OpenRouter's routing layer disallowed max_tokens=1, complicating TTFT measurement), and the responsible-disclosure process with OpenRouter was left unresolved (acknowledged but no substantive response after repo-access invitation).

datasets_used: Synthetic prompts: 4,096-token sequences of space-separated ASCII letters with a 95% prefix fraction (3,891 shared tokens / 205 differing tokens), generated by the authors, not a named external dataset
quantitative_results: Direct API, Groq same-account: KS p=3.8e-11, miss TTFT mean 0.460s, hit TTFT mean 0.766s, cache detected via timing
Direct API, Groq cross-account: KS p=8.3e-03, no detection
Direct API, Fireworks same-account: KS p=3.8e-11, cache detected via timing
Direct API, Fireworks cross-account: KS p=6.1e-01, no detection
Direct API, OpenAI same-account: KS p=1.3e-01 (timing not significant), detected via metadata disclosure
Direct API, OpenAI cross-account: KS p=6.9e-01, no detection
OpenRouter default, Groq same-account: KS p=2.4e-03, detected via metadata (100% cache-hit-rate disclosure)
OpenRouter default, Groq cross-account: KS p=1.7e-03, detected via metadata (100% cache-hit-rate disclosure, all reported samples exceeding 90% prefix threshold)
OpenRouter default, Fireworks same-account: KS p=3.3e-13, detected via timing
OpenRouter default, Fireworks cross-account: KS p=4.08e-15 (KS stat 0.364), the study's strongest cross-account cache-sharing signal, detected via timing
OpenRouter default, OpenAI same-account: KS p=4.5e-03, detected via metadata
OpenRouter default, OpenAI cross-account: KS p=6.1e-01 (timing not significant), detected via metadata at a 4.8% cache-hit rate on cross-account requests
OpenRouter BYOK, Groq same-account: KS p=4.0e-01, detected via metadata; cross-account: KS p=6.9e-01, not detected
OpenRouter BYOK, Fireworks same-account: KS p=3.7e-47, detected via timing and metadata; cross-account: KS p=6.1e-01, not detected
OpenRouter BYOK, OpenAI same-account: KS p=2.9e-01, detected via metadata; cross-account: KS p=1.6e-01, not detected
Overall test matrix: 6 scenarios x 3 providers = 18 configurations, 250 sample pairs per scenario, ~9,000 total API requests over Nov 12-Dec 8, 2025
baselines_compared: Direct provider API access (native per-account/per-organization credentials) used as the isolation-correctness baseline against which OpenRouter default and BYOK routing modes are compared

Detection methodology (KS test at p<1e-8 threshold) adopted directly from Gu et al. [1] 'Auditing Prompt Caching in Language Model APIs' (ICML 2025), which the paper cites as the source of the timing side-channel attack technique being applied to a new gateway setting
